case_name: Earneka Wiggins v. Hackensack Meridian Health

summary.brief: Earneka Wiggins v. Hackensack Meridian Health. Medical malpractice case involving surgery orthopaedic.

evidence_index.text: Plaintiffs, the administrators of the Estate of April Carden, filed a medical malpractice complaint against defendants Hackensack Meridian Health d/b/a JFK University Medical Center (JFK), Alok Goyal, M.D., and South Plainfield Primary Care (SPPC) (collectively, defendants).
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Plaintiffs allege that Carden’s death was directly attributable to Allopurinol prescribed by Dr. Goyal, that Dr. Goyal’s negligence caused Carden’s death, and that JFK and SPPC were vicariously liable because Dr. Goyal was an “agent, servant, or employee” of both.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Plaintiffs provided each defendant with an AOM from Dr. Stella Jones Fitzgibbons, who is board certified by the American Board of Internal Medicine.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The trial judge denied the motions to dismiss the complaint. He found “that the ‘care and treatment at issue’ was the prescribing of Allopurinol, and the ‘care or treatment at issue involves internal [medicine].’”
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: The Appellate Division granted defendants’ motion for leave to appeal from the orders denying their motions to dismiss and for reconsideration and reversed those orders, concluding that plaintiffs were required to “serve an AOM from a physician board certified in each of [Dr. Goyal’s] specialties.”
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: Under Buck, when “the treatment involved may fall within [a] physician’s multiple specialty areas” -- as is the case here -- “an [AOM] from a physician specializing in either area will suffice.”
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

evidence_index.text: We therefore reverse the Appellate Division’s judgment, reinstate the trial court orders denying defendants’ motions to dismiss and motions for reconsideration, and remand for further proceedings.
evidence_index.speaker: Court finding

initial_state.history_of_present_illness: In 2016, Carden experienced a severe allergic reaction to Tramadol and/or Allopurinol and was diagnosed with Stevens-Johnson Syndrome (SJS).

focused_exam.skin: Painful blisters and lesions on the skin and mucous membranes

initial_state.initial_working_problem: Severe allergic reaction potentially due to medication

requestables.name: Blood tests
requestables.description: To assess for systemic involvement and confirm diagnosis

requestables.name: Allergy/Immunology consult
requestables.description: Consult to evaluate and manage drug allergies

timeline_phases.name: Initial Presentation
timeline_phases.description: Patient presents with severe allergic reaction

timeline_phases.name: Subsequent Medical Treatment
timeline_phases.description: Patient receives treatment for allergic reaction

decision_points.clinical_context: Patient with known allergy to Allopurinol prescribed the medication again
decision_points.prompt: Should Allopurinol be prescribed to this patient?

options.description: Do not prescribe Allopurinol due to known allergy
options.clinical_reasoning: Prescribing a medication known to cause severe allergic reaction is contraindicated.
Defendant prescribed Allopurinol despite known allergy.

expected_action_court.description: Allopurinol should not have been prescribed

explanation.why_defendant_wrong: Prescribing Allopurinol to a patient with a known severe allergy to the drug is a deviation from standard care.
explanation.what_should_have_happened: The patient should have been prescribed an alternative medication or managed without Allopurinol.
explanation.legal_standard_applied: Standard of care as established by expert testimony and known medical guidelines.

patient_outcome.description: Patient passed away from cardiopulmonary arrest due to multiple organ failure, bacteremia, and Stevens-Johnson Syndrome.

key_findings: Breach of duty in prescribing Allopurinol despite known allergy
Causation established - prescription led to fatal outcome

malpractice_determination.counterfactual: Avoiding Allopurinol would have prevented the fatal reaction

complications.complication: Cardiopulmonary arrest due to multiple organ failure, bacteremia, and Stevens-Johnson Syndrome

allegations.allegation: Failure to recognize and avoid prescribing Allopurinol despite known allergy

court_findings.finding: Defendant breached duty of care by prescribing Allopurinol
